meetingTopic: Interview: John Doe — Senior React Engineer

questions.competencyName: React Basics — nested components, JSX, conditional rendering
questions.questionText: Can you walk me through how you decide between using inline conditional rendering with && versus a ternary operator or an if statement in JSX?
followUpHints: What edge cases might make one approach better than another?
How do you handle complex conditions without cluttering JSX?

questions.questionText: What are the trade-offs you consider when choosing between CSS Modules, styled-components, and utility-first frameworks like Tailwind CSS for a React project?
followUpHints: How do you handle dynamic styles with each approach?
What about performance implications?

questions.questionText: Describe a situation where you had to manage complex state with useState and side effects with useEffect. How did you structure the component to avoid unnecessary re-renders or stale closures?
followUpHints: How do you handle cleanup in useEffect?
When would you use useReducer instead of multiple useState calls?

questions.questionText: Explain the role of the key prop in React lists and what can go wrong if you use array index as a key. Can you give an example where using index causes a bug?
followUpHints: How would you generate stable keys for dynamic lists?
What about lists that don't have a unique id?

questions.questionText: How would you design a form in React that needs to validate inputs on blur and submit, and then send data to an API? Walk me through your component structure and state management choices.
followUpHints: How do you handle async validation (e.g., checking username availability)?
What libraries have you used for form handling and why?

questions.questionText: Describe your approach to testing a React component that relies on an API call. How do you use React Testing Library to mock the fetch and verify the loading, success, and error states?
followUpHints: What is your opinion on testing implementation details vs behavior?
How do you test custom hooks?

questions.questionText: Can you explain how error boundaries work in React and what limitations they have? How would you implement a fallback UI for a component that throws an error during rendering?
followUpHints: Can error boundaries catch errors in event handlers or async code? If not, how would you handle those?
How do you log errors from error boundaries?

questions.questionText: When building a Next.js application, how do you decide between SSR, SSG, and ISR for a given page? Can you walk through a real example where you chose one over the others and why?
followUpHints: How does data freshness versus build time factor into your decision?
What are the trade-offs for SEO and user experience?

questions.competencyName: React design patterns (Custom Hooks, HOC, etc.)
questions.questionText: Compare custom hooks and higher-order components for sharing logic across components. When would you choose one over the other, and what are the pitfalls of each?
followUpHints: How do you handle dependency injection with hooks?
Can you give an example of a custom hook you built to encapsulate complex logic?

questions.questionText: Describe a scenario where you would use Context API combined with useReducer for state management. How do you prevent unnecessary re-renders when the context value changes?
followUpHints: What are the performance implications of using context for frequently updating state?
How would you split contexts to avoid re-rendering unrelated components?

questions.questionText: What are some practical use cases for useRef beyond accessing DOM elements? Can you explain how useRef can help with storing mutable values without causing re-renders?
followUpHints: How do you use useRef to track previous state or props?
Can you give an example where useRef is used to integrate with imperative third-party libraries?

questions.questionText: How do you decide when to use useMemo or useCallback in a React component? Can you give an example where incorrect usage actually hurts performance rather than helping?
followUpHints: What are the costs of memoization (memory, comparison)?
How do you profile to know if memoization is needed?

questions.questionText: Explain how you would implement code splitting with React.lazy and Suspense for route-based splitting in a React app. What are the benefits and potential issues?
followUpHints: How do you handle loading states and error boundaries with lazy routes?
When would you choose a multi-page app (MPA) over a single-page app (SPA)?

questions.competencyName: State management PoV (Redux, RTK, react-query, etc.)
questions.questionText: Given a new React project, how do you decide whether to use Redux Toolkit, React Query, or just React Context for state management? What factors influence your choice?
followUpHints: How do you handle server state vs client state?
What are the trade-offs of using a global store versus local component state?

questions.competencyName: npm registries, dotenv, logging best practices
questions.questionText: How do you manage environment variables in a React application across different environments (development, staging, production)? What are the security considerations?
followUpHints: How do you handle secrets that should not be in the client bundle?
What logging practices do you follow for debugging production React apps?

questions.questionText: Can you describe the 12-factor app methodology and how it applies to building Node.js microservices? Which factors do you find most critical for maintainability?
followUpHints: How do you handle configuration management across services?
What are the trade-offs of microservices vs monoliths for a Node.js backend?

questions.questionText: Explain the lifecycle of an HTTP request in a Node.js server. How do you handle different HTTP status codes and error responses in a RESTful way?
followUpHints: How do you structure error handling middleware?
What is the significance of status codes like 201, 204, 401, 403, 500?

questions.questionText: How do you design a RESTful API for a resource like 'users'? What endpoints, HTTP methods, and response formats would you use? How do you document it with Swagger?
followUpHints: How do you handle pagination, filtering, and sorting?
What is your experience with API versioning?

questions.questionText: Compare WebSockets and Server-Sent Events for real-time communication in a Node.js application. When would you choose one over the other?
followUpHints: How do you handle reconnection and message ordering?
What are the scalability considerations for each?

questions.questionText: How do you decide between using an ORM like Prisma or Sequelize versus writing raw SQL queries in a Node.js application? What are the trade-offs?
followUpHints: How do you handle database migrations?
What role does Redis play in your architecture for caching or session management?

questions.questionText: Compare Express, Fastify, and NestJS for building a Node.js API. What factors would lead you to choose one over the others for a new project?
followUpHints: How do each of them handle middleware and error handling?
What about performance and developer experience?

questions.questionText: What are the key differences between Apollo Server, AWS AppSync, and Hasura for building a GraphQL API? In what scenarios would you recommend each?
followUpHints: How do they handle real-time subscriptions?
What about authorization and data source integration?

questions.questionText: Describe different caching strategies for GraphQL APIs (e.g., at the resolver level, using DataLoader, CDN caching, or persisted queries). How do you decide which to use?
followUpHints: How does caching differ from REST?
What are the challenges with caching GraphQL responses due to dynamic queries?

questions.questionText: Explain how DataLoader helps with batching and caching in GraphQL resolvers. Can you also describe what custom scalars are and when you might define one?
followUpHints: How does introspection work and when would you disable it in production?
What are the trade-offs of using custom scalars?

questions.questionText: How do you handle errors in GraphQL? Compare returning errors in the 'errors' array versus throwing exceptions. How do you pass arguments to resolvers and validate them?
followUpHints: How do you handle partial success scenarios?
What libraries do you use for input validation?

questions.questionText: Walk me through how you would design a GraphQL schema for a blogging platform (users, posts, comments). How do you structure resolvers and use GraphQL Playground for testing?
followUpHints: How do you handle authentication and authorization in resolvers?
What are best practices for organizing schema files?

questions.questionText: Compare Apollo Client and Relay for managing GraphQL data on the client side. What are the key differences and when would you choose one over the other?
followUpHints: How do they handle caching and optimistic updates?
What about pagination and normalized cache?

header.domainFeedbackSummary: John demonstrated strong senior-level React expertise, particularly in rendering patterns (SSR/SSG/ISR/RSC), performance optimization (virtualization, memoization), and state management philosophy (server vs client state). He articulated clear trade-offs for useMemo/useCallback, error boundaries, and testing strategies. His hands-on exercise was not evaluated in the transcript, but his conceptual answers were deep and practical. Weaknesses include limited Node.js depth (only Express/Fastify experience, no mention of streaming or databases) and GraphQL exposure (only one project, no discussion of caching strategies or clients beyond Apollo). Overall, he is a strong hire for a Senior Experience Engineer role.
header.teachableSkillGapDetails: Node.js backend depth (streaming, databases, ORMs) and GraphQL advanced topics (caching, batching, clients) could be developed further.

competencies.feedbackDetails: John explained conditional rendering trade-offs (&& vs ternary vs if) and handling complex conditions without cluttering JSX, demonstrating deep practical experience.
John discussed CSS Modules, styled-components, and Tailwind trade-offs, including dynamic styles and performance implications, showing strong practical knowledge.
John described managing complex state with useState and useEffect, avoiding stale closures, and using useReducer for non-trivial transitions. He also mentioned cleanup in useEffect.
John explained the key prop role, pitfalls of using array index, and generating stable keys for dynamic lists, including lists without unique ids.
John described using react-hook-form with zod for validation, handling async validation, and accessibility considerations for forms.
John follows the testing trophy approach, uses RTL with MSW for API mocking, and avoids testing implementation details. He also mentioned testing custom hooks.
John explained error boundaries limitations (no async/event handler catching), placement strategies, and implementing useful fallback UIs with retry buttons.
John gave a clear decision framework for SSR/SSG/ISR/CSR based on content freshness and personalization, and mentioned RSC mixing in Next.js 14.
John compared custom hooks and HOCs, explaining when to choose each and pitfalls. He gave an example of a polymorphic Button using TypeScript generics.
John described using Context with useReducer for complex state, splitting contexts to avoid re-renders, and providing stable context values.
John explained useRef for mutable values without re-renders, DOM access, and tracking previous state. He distinguished it from regular variables.
John gave a nuanced view on memoization, warning against overuse and explaining costs. He advocates profiling before memoizing.
John discussed React.lazy and Suspense for route splitting, handling loading states and error boundaries, and when to choose MPA over SPA.
John clearly separates server state (react-query) from client state (useState/Context/Zustand/Redux) and explains trade-offs for each.
John mentioned managing env variables with dotenv, security considerations (no secrets in client bundle), and logging practices for production debugging.
John mentioned 12-factor app principles (config from env, stateless processes, logs as streams) but did not elaborate deeply on microservices trade-offs.
John did not discuss HTTP lifecycle or status codes in detail; his Node experience is limited to framework usage.
John mentioned REST API design with Express/Fastify but did not cover pagination, filtering, or API versioning.

competencies.proficiency: Able to explain concepts in depth
competencies.feedbackDetails: John compared WebSockets and SSE, explaining use cases, reconnection, and scalability considerations. He used socket.io for WebSocket rooms.
John mentioned Prisma with Postgres but did not discuss ORM vs raw SQL trade-offs, migrations, or Redis.

competencies.proficiency: Able to explain concepts in depth
competencies.feedbackDetails: John compared Express, Fastify, and NestJS, explaining performance, developer experience, and when to choose each.
John mentioned Apollo Server with Prisma but did not compare with AppSync or Hasura.
John mentioned InMemoryCache and fetchPolicy but did not discuss caching strategies in depth or challenges.
John mentioned dataloader for batching but did not discuss introspection or custom scalars.
John mentioned schema design with Prisma but did not walk through a full schema or resolver structure.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Hi, thanks for joining today. Could you start by telling me a bit about your background and the React work you've been doing recently?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Sure. I've been working with React for about five years. Most recently at a fintech I led the rewrite of our trading dashboard from a CRA setup to a Next.js 14 app router project. We had a hard requirement around streaming the initial paint under 1.5 seconds even on mid-tier devices, so I leaned heavily on RSC and Suspense boundaries. Before that I spent two years on a design system team building reusable components published to a private npm registry, including a polymorphic Button using TypeScript generics and an Input that handled controlled and uncontrolled modes properly.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Good. Let's start with rendering patterns. Walk me through when you'd reach for SSR versus SSG versus client-only rendering, and where ISR fits in.

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: I think of it as a spectrum keyed off of two things: how often the content changes and how personalized it is. SSG when the content is the same for every user and changes rarely, like marketing pages or docs — you get the cheapest cache hit at the CDN edge. ISR is the same idea but with a TTL so editors don't have to redeploy; you trade staleness for ergonomics. SSR when the page is per-user or per-request — auth-gated dashboards, search result pages — you pay for compute on every request but you avoid client-side flicker. CSR when interactivity dominates and SEO doesn't matter, like a logged-in workspace. In Next 14 it gets blurrier because RSC lets you mix server components for the shell and client components for the interactive bits, which is usually the right answer for a real app.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Nice. How would you handle a list of 10,000 rows in React without killing performance?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Don't render 10,000 DOM nodes. Virtualize with react-window or TanStack Virtual — they render only the visible window plus a small overscan. You also have to give each row a stable key, not the array index, because if the data reorders React will reuse the wrong DOM. For the data side I'd memoize the row component with React.memo and pass a stable callback via useCallback so the row doesn't re-render on parent updates. If the rows are heavy I'd also profile with the React DevTools profiler to see if a context update is forcing a re-render and split the context if so.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Tell me about useMemo and useCallback. When are they worth it and when are they noise?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: The honest answer is most people overuse them. useMemo is worth it when the computation is genuinely expensive — a large sort, a tree flatten — or when the referential identity of the value matters because it's a dep of another hook or a memoized child. useCallback is the same: only useful if the function is passed to a memoized child or used as a hook dep. Wrapping every handler in useCallback is cargo culting and actually costs you memory. The mental model I use is: don't memoize until you have a profiler trace showing a real problem, except at the boundary of an obviously memoized component.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: What about state management in a real app? Redux, Zustand, react-query — how do you choose?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: I split state into server state and client state. Server state — anything that came from an API — goes in react-query or RTK Query because they handle caching, dedupe, background refetch, and stale-while-revalidate out of the box. For client state I default to useState colocated with the component. If two distant components need it I lift to a Context, and only reach for Zustand or Redux Toolkit when there's complex cross-cutting state with derived selectors. Redux is overkill for most apps in 2026, but it's still defensible if you need devtools time travel for a complex domain like a trading book or a CAD tool.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Let's go deeper on hooks. Explain useRef and the difference between useRef and a regular variable in a component.

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: A regular variable gets re-initialized on every render. useRef gives you a mutable container that persists across renders without triggering a re-render when you change it. Two common uses: holding a DOM node so you can imperatively focus an input, and stashing a mutable value like a setTimeout id or a previous-value snapshot for comparison. The key thing is mutating ref.current does NOT cause a re-render — if you want a re-render when something changes, that's state, not a ref.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Have you used Context with useReducer for state? When would you reach for that over plain useState?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Yes. The pattern shines when you have a state object with several related fields and the transitions are non-trivial — like a form with multi-step validation, or a wizard. The reducer documents the legal transitions in one place. I'll wrap it in a context with a stable value so consumers don't re-render unnecessarily, and I'll often split the dispatch into its own provider so components that only dispatch don't subscribe to state.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: What about error boundaries? Where do you place them and what do they catch versus not catch?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Error boundaries catch errors in the render phase, lifecycle methods, and constructors of children below them. They do not catch errors in event handlers, async code, or server-side rendering — for those you wrap with try/catch or use the onError callback. Placement-wise I put one at the route level so a crashed page doesn't take down the whole app, and a finer-grained one around risky widgets like a third-party charting library. The fallback should be useful — a retry button and a reset key, not just "Something went wrong."

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: How do you handle forms in React? Controlled vs uncontrolled, validation libraries?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: For anything non-trivial I use react-hook-form because it's uncontrolled by default which keeps re-renders to a minimum, and the resolver pattern plays well with zod schemas — I get one source of truth for the type and the runtime validation. For tiny one-or-two-field forms I'll just use controlled useState. Native HTML5 validation gets you a long way for required and type=email; I lean on it before reaching for a library. For accessibility I make sure each input has a real label, aria-invalid on error, and a live region for the error message.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Walk me through testing. RTL, Jest, what's your philosophy?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: I follow the testing trophy from Kent C. Dodds — heavy on integration, light on unit, and a small e2e layer with Playwright for critical paths like checkout or login. RTL queries by role and accessible name, which doubles as an accessibility check. I avoid testing implementation details like the names of useState setters or specific class names. For mocks I use MSW at the network boundary instead of mocking fetch directly, because that lets the same handlers run in tests and in Storybook. Coverage targets are a smell to me — I focus on whether the critical user flows are covered.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Tell me about TypeScript with React. Have you written generic components or used advanced types?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Yes. The polymorphic Button I mentioned is a good example — it takes an "as" prop that lets you render as a button, a link, or any element, and the rest of the props are typed against that element. That involves a ComponentPropsWithoutRef plus a generic with a default of "button". I use conditional types for things like making a prop required only when another prop is set — a discriminated union is usually cleaner though. tsconfig-wise I always turn on strict, noUncheckedIndexedAccess, and exactOptionalPropertyTypes. Those three catch the most real bugs.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Let's talk performance budgets. What metrics do you watch and how do you fix regressions?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: I anchor on the Core Web Vitals — LCP, CLS, INP since it replaced FID. For a React app, LCP is usually about how fast the hero element renders and whether it's blocked by JS hydration. I'll check the waterfall in Lighthouse or WebPageTest, look for render-blocking scripts, preload the LCP image, and split routes with React.lazy plus Suspense. CLS is usually missing dimensions on images or late-loading fonts — I fix that with width/height attributes and font-display swap with a sensible fallback metric override. INP is event handler latency — yieldy work with scheduler.yield, debounce expensive handlers, and move heavy compute to a worker if needed.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Accessibility — what's your real-world approach beyond axe and Lighthouse?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Automated tools catch maybe 30% of issues. Real wins come from keyboard testing — tab through the whole flow, make sure focus is visible and lands where you'd expect, that modal traps focus and returns it on close. Screen reader testing with VoiceOver or NVDA at least once per major flow. Color contrast against actual content, not just buttons. ARIA only when there isn't a native element — a button is always better than a div with role=button. I also try to ship a "reduced motion" code path because the prefers-reduced-motion media query is easy to honor and a real accessibility win.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Security on the frontend — what's on your radar?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: XSS first — never dangerouslySetInnerHTML on user content without sanitizing through DOMPurify; React's default escaping handles most cases. CSP headers set tight, with nonces if you have inline scripts. CSRF — same-site cookies and tokens for state-changing requests. Don't store auth tokens in localStorage if you can avoid it; httpOnly cookies are safer against XSS exfiltration. For third-party scripts I use SRI hashes. And I never log PII to the browser console or analytics.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Tooling — Vite vs webpack, and what does your lint/format setup look like?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Vite for new projects — dev startup is night and day, native ESM is the right primitive. Webpack still wins for very complex enterprise pipelines with custom loaders. For lint I use ESLint with typescript-eslint, the react and react-hooks plugins, and jsx-a11y. Prettier for formatting, with the eslint-config-prettier wired in so they don't fight. Husky plus lint-staged for pre-commit hooks. I also run typecheck in CI as a separate job so PR feedback is fast.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Let's pivot to Node briefly. Have you built REST APIs in Node? What framework?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Yes. Most of my Node work has been Express historically, and recently fastify because the schema-driven validation with JSON Schema is fast and gives you OpenAPI docs essentially for free. For a clean architecture I lean on the 12-factor principles — config from env, stateless processes, logs as event streams. I've used Nest for one project but found it heavy for what was a small team. I'd reach for it again only if I were on a 15-plus engineer org that needed the discipline.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Have you worked with WebSockets or SSE?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Both. WebSockets when the protocol needs to be truly bidirectional — a live collaboration cursor or a chat. SSE when it's server-push-only — a notifications feed, a deployment progress tracker — because it gives you reconnection and event IDs for free over plain HTTP, and it plays nicer with HTTP/2 and corporate proxies. I've used socket.io for the WebSocket layer when I needed rooms and reconnection out of the box.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: What about GraphQL — have you used it in production?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Yes, on a product catalog. We used Apollo Server on the backend with a Postgres-backed Prisma schema, and Apollo Client on the React side with the InMemoryCache. The wins were obvious for the frontend — request exactly what you need, no overfetching, typed end-to-end. The N+1 problem was the main pain point — we fixed it with dataloader to batch. Caching strategy was a per-type policy in the InMemoryCache; we used fetchPolicy 'cache-and-network' for the hot paths.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Last question — what's your take on Module Federation and micro-frontends?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: I'm skeptical by default. Most teams that adopt micro-frontends do it for organizational reasons not technical ones, and they end up duplicating runtime, fighting version skew, and slowing the boot time of the host app. Module Federation is a fine tool if you genuinely need independent deploy cadences across teams and the shared chunks are well-managed. I've shipped one production MFE with five domains using Module Federation 2, shared React singletons, and a tight contract on the design system version. It worked, but the operational complexity was real — versioning, type sharing, runtime errors when a remote was unavailable.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Great. Anything you want to ask me or anything we didn't cover?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Two quick things — what does your release cadence look like, and how does the team handle on-call rotations for the frontend? Also curious how design and engineering collaborate at the spec stage.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: We ship multiple times a day behind feature flags, on-call is two-week rotations shared across full-stack engineers, and we have a weekly design-engineering sync where we triage the next two sprints together. Thanks for the time — we'll be in touch this week.

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Thanks, appreciated the conversation.

transcript.speaker: Medha
transcript.text: 👋 Hello and welcome! I'm Medha, your AI interviewer. This conversation will be analyzed by an AI assistant and a structured probe form will be generated for the hiring panel. The session is being recorded and transcribed for that purpose. By continuing, you consent to this process. Data retention: 30 days.

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Got it, thanks — happy to proceed.

transcript.speaker: Medha
transcript.text: Question 1. Walk us through when you'd reach for SSR vs SSG vs CSR vs ISR, and where RSC fits in.

transcript.speaker: Medha
transcript.text: Question 2. How would you render a list of 10,000 rows in React without killing performance? What about useMemo and useCallback — when are they worth it?

transcript.speaker: Medha
transcript.text: Question 3. For the hands-on round, please use this CoderPad link: Shopping Cart Exercise . You have 20 minutes — I'll see your code at the end.

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Opening it now — will let you know when I'm done.

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Done — submitted.

transcript.speaker: Medha
transcript.text: Thanks. Final question: tell us about accessibility — what's your real-world approach beyond Lighthouse and axe?

What are the downsides of putting everything in Redux?

header.sectionsToBeTrainedOn: GraphQL — caching strategies, GraphQL — batching, introspection, scalars, GraphQL — errors and arguments, GraphQL — clients
header.domainFeedbackSummary: The candidate demonstrated strong React fundamentals, including deep knowledge of rendering patterns (SSR/SSG/ISR/CSR), performance optimization (virtualization, memoization), and state management (react-query, Context, useReducer). They showed hands-on experience with Next.js, TypeScript generics, and testing with RTL and MSW. Weaknesses were evident in GraphQL: they mentioned using Apollo Client and DataLoader but lacked depth on caching strategies, batching, introspection, custom scalars, error handling, and client comparisons. Node.js knowledge was adequate but not deep, with only basic Express/fastify experience and no demonstrated expertise in streaming, databases, or 12-factor architecture. Overall, the candidate is a strong senior frontend engineer with a gap in GraphQL that can be addressed through training.
header.teachableSkillGapDetails: GraphQL: caching strategies, batching, introspection, scalars, error handling, client comparisons (Apollo vs Relay). Node.js: deeper architectural concepts, streaming, databases/ORMs/Redis.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Hi, thanks for joining today. Could you start by telling me a bit about your background and the React work you've been doing recently?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Sure. I've been working with React for about five years. Most recently at a fintech I led the rewrite of our trading dashboard from a CRA setup to a Next.js 14 app router project. We had a hard requirement around streaming the initial paint under 1.5 seconds even on mid-tier devices, so I leaned heavily on RSC and Suspense boundaries. Before that I spent two years on a design system team building reusable components published to a private npm registry, including a polymorphic Button using TypeScript generics and an Input that handled controlled and uncontrolled modes properly.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Good. Let's start with rendering patterns. Walk me through when you'd reach for SSR versus SSG versus client-only rendering, and where ISR fits in.

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: I think of it as a spectrum keyed off of two things: how often the content changes and how personalized it is. SSG when the content is the same for every user and changes rarely, like marketing pages or docs — you get the cheapest cache hit at the CDN edge. ISR is the same idea but with a TTL so editors don't have to redeploy; you trade staleness for ergonomics. SSR when the page is per-user or per-request — auth-gated dashboards, search result pages — you pay for compute on every request but you avoid client-side flicker. CSR when interactivity dominates and SEO doesn't matter, like a logged-in workspace. In Next 14 it gets blurrier because RSC lets you mix server components for the shell and client components for the interactive bits, which is usually the right answer for a real app.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Nice. How would you handle a list of 10,000 rows in React without killing performance?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Don't render 10,000 DOM nodes. Virtualize with react-window or TanStack Virtual — they render only the visible window plus a small overscan. You also have to give each row a stable key, not the array index, because if the data reorders React will reuse the wrong DOM. For the data side I'd memoize the row component with React.memo and pass a stable callback via useCallback so the row doesn't re-render on parent updates. If the rows are heavy I'd also profile with the React DevTools profiler to see if a context update is forcing a re-render and split the context if so.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Tell me about useMemo and useCallback. When are they worth it and when are they noise?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: The honest answer is most people overuse them. useMemo is worth it when the computation is genuinely expensive — a large sort, a tree flatten — or when the referential identity of the value matters because it's a dep of another hook or a memoized child. useCallback is the same: only useful if the function is passed to a memoized child or used as a hook dep. Wrapping every handler in useCallback is cargo culting and actually costs you memory. The mental model I use is: don't memoize until you have a profiler trace showing a real problem, except at the boundary of an obviously memoized component.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: What about state management in a real app? Redux, Zustand, react-query — how do you choose?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: I split state into server state and client state. Server state — anything that came from an API — goes in react-query or RTK Query because they handle caching, dedupe, background refetch, and stale-while-revalidate out of the box. For client state I default to useState colocated with the component. If two distant components need it I lift to a Context, and only reach for Zustand or Redux Toolkit when there's complex cross-cutting state with derived selectors. Redux is overkill for most apps in 2026, but it's still defensible if you need devtools time travel for a complex domain like a trading book or a CAD tool.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Let's go deeper on hooks. Explain useRef and the difference between useRef and a regular variable in a component.

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: A regular variable gets re-initialized on every render. useRef gives you a mutable container that persists across renders without triggering a re-render when you change it. Two common uses: holding a DOM node so you can imperatively focus an input, and stashing a mutable value like a setTimeout id or a previous-value snapshot for comparison. The key thing is mutating ref.current does NOT cause a re-render — if you want a re-render when something changes, that's state, not a ref.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Have you used Context with useReducer for state? When would you reach for that over plain useState?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Yes. The pattern shines when you have a state object with several related fields and the transitions are non-trivial — like a form with multi-step validation, or a wizard. The reducer documents the legal transitions in one place. I'll wrap it in a context with a stable value so consumers don't re-render unnecessarily, and I'll often split the dispatch into its own provider so components that only dispatch don't subscribe to state.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: What about error boundaries? Where do you place them and what do they catch versus not catch?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Error boundaries catch errors in the render phase, lifecycle methods, and constructors of children below them. They do not catch errors in event handlers, async code, or server-side rendering — for those you wrap with try/catch or use the onError callback. Placement-wise I put one at the route level so a crashed page doesn't take down the whole app, and a finer-grained one around risky widgets like a third-party charting library. The fallback should be useful — a retry button and a reset key, not just "Something went wrong."

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: How do you handle forms in React? Controlled vs uncontrolled, validation libraries?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: For anything non-trivial I use react-hook-form because it's uncontrolled by default which keeps re-renders to a minimum, and the resolver pattern plays well with zod schemas — I get one source of truth for the type and the runtime validation. For tiny one-or-two-field forms I'll just use controlled useState. Native HTML5 validation gets you a long way for required and type=email; I lean on it before reaching for a library. For accessibility I make sure each input has a real label, aria-invalid on error, and a live region for the error message.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Walk me through testing. RTL, Jest, what's your philosophy?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: I follow the testing trophy from Kent C. Dodds — heavy on integration, light on unit, and a small e2e layer with Playwright for critical paths like checkout or login. RTL queries by role and accessible name, which doubles as an accessibility check. I avoid testing implementation details like the names of useState setters or specific class names. For mocks I use MSW at the network boundary instead of mocking fetch directly, because that lets the same handlers run in tests and in Storybook. Coverage targets are a smell to me — I focus on whether the critical user flows are covered.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Tell me about TypeScript with React. Have you written generic components or used advanced types?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Yes. The polymorphic Button I mentioned is a good example — it takes an "as" prop that lets you render as a button, a link, or any element, and the rest of the props are typed against that element. That involves a ComponentPropsWithoutRef plus a generic with a default of "button". I use conditional types for things like making a prop required only when another prop is set — a discriminated union is usually cleaner though. tsconfig-wise I always turn on strict, noUncheckedIndexedAccess, and exactOptionalPropertyTypes. Those three catch the most real bugs.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Let's talk performance budgets. What metrics do you watch and how do you fix regressions?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: I anchor on the Core Web Vitals — LCP, CLS, INP since it replaced FID. For a React app, LCP is usually about how fast the hero element renders and whether it's blocked by JS hydration. I'll check the waterfall in Lighthouse or WebPageTest, look for render-blocking scripts, preload the LCP image, and split routes with React.lazy plus Suspense. CLS is usually missing dimensions on images or late-loading fonts — I fix that with width/height attributes and font-display swap with a sensible fallback metric override. INP is event handler latency — yieldy work with scheduler.yield, debounce expensive handlers, and move heavy compute to a worker if needed.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Accessibility — what's your real-world approach beyond axe and Lighthouse?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Automated tools catch maybe 30% of issues. Real wins come from keyboard testing — tab through the whole flow, make sure focus is visible and lands where you'd expect, that modal traps focus and returns it on close. Screen reader testing with VoiceOver or NVDA at least once per major flow. Color contrast against actual content, not just buttons. ARIA only when there isn't a native element — a button is always better than a div with role=button. I also try to ship a "reduced motion" code path because the prefers-reduced-motion media query is easy to honor and a real accessibility win.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Security on the frontend — what's on your radar?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: XSS first — never dangerouslySetInnerHTML on user content without sanitizing through DOMPurify; React's default escaping handles most cases. CSP headers set tight, with nonces if you have inline scripts. CSRF — same-site cookies and tokens for state-changing requests. Don't store auth tokens in localStorage if you can avoid it; httpOnly cookies are safer against XSS exfiltration. For third-party scripts I use SRI hashes. And I never log PII to the browser console or analytics.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Tooling — Vite vs webpack, and what does your lint/format setup look like?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Vite for new projects — dev startup is night and day, native ESM is the right primitive. Webpack still wins for very complex enterprise pipelines with custom loaders. For lint I use ESLint with typescript-eslint, the react and react-hooks plugins, and jsx-a11y. Prettier for formatting, with the eslint-config-prettier wired in so they don't fight. Husky plus lint-staged for pre-commit hooks. I also run typecheck in CI as a separate job so PR feedback is fast.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Let's pivot to Node briefly. Have you built REST APIs in Node? What framework?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Yes. Most of my Node work has been Express historically, and recently fastify because the schema-driven validation with JSON Schema is fast and gives you OpenAPI docs essentially for free. For a clean architecture I lean on the 12-factor principles — config from env, stateless processes, logs as event streams. I've used Nest for one project but found it heavy for what was a small team. I'd reach for it again only if I were on a 15-plus engineer org that needed the discipline.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Have you worked with WebSockets or SSE?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Both. WebSockets when the protocol needs to be truly bidirectional — a live collaboration cursor or a chat. SSE when it's server-push-only — a notifications feed, a deployment progress tracker — because it gives you reconnection and event IDs for free over plain HTTP, and it plays nicer with HTTP/2 and corporate proxies. I've used socket.io for the WebSocket layer when I needed rooms and reconnection out of the box.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: What about GraphQL — have you used it in production?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Yes, on a product catalog. We used Apollo Server on the backend with a Postgres-backed Prisma schema, and Apollo Client on the React side with the InMemoryCache. The wins were obvious for the frontend — request exactly what you need, no overfetching, typed end-to-end. The N+1 problem was the main pain point — we fixed it with dataloader to batch. Caching strategy was a per-type policy in the InMemoryCache; we used fetchPolicy 'cache-and-network' for the hot paths.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Last question — what's your take on Module Federation and micro-frontends?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: I'm skeptical by default. Most teams that adopt micro-frontends do it for organizational reasons not technical ones, and they end up duplicating runtime, fighting version skew, and slowing the boot time of the host app. Module Federation is a fine tool if you genuinely need independent deploy cadences across teams and the shared chunks are well-managed. I've shipped one production MFE with five domains using Module Federation 2, shared React singletons, and a tight contract on the design system version. It worked, but the operational complexity was real — versioning, type sharing, runtime errors when a remote was unavailable.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Great. Anything you want to ask me or anything we didn't cover?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Two quick things — what does your release cadence look like, and how does the team handle on-call rotations for the frontend? Also curious how design and engineering collaborate at the spec stage.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: We ship multiple times a day behind feature flags, on-call is two-week rotations shared across full-stack engineers, and we have a weekly design-engineering sync where we triage the next two sprints together. Thanks for the time — we'll be in touch this week.

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Thanks, appreciated the conversation.

transcript.speaker: Medha
transcript.text: 👋 Hello and welcome! I'm Medha, your AI interviewer. This conversation will be analyzed by an AI assistant and a structured probe form will be generated for the hiring panel. The session is being recorded and transcribed for that purpose. By continuing, you consent to this process. Data retention: 30 days.

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Got it, thanks — happy to proceed.

transcript.speaker: Medha
transcript.text: Question 1. Walk us through when you'd reach for SSR vs SSG vs CSR vs ISR, and where RSC fits in.

transcript.speaker: Medha
transcript.text: Question 2. How would you render a list of 10,000 rows in React without killing performance? What about useMemo and useCallback — when are they worth it?

transcript.speaker: Medha
transcript.text: Question 3. For the hands-on round, please use this CoderPad link: Shopping Cart Exercise . You have 20 minutes — I'll see your code at the end.

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Opening it now — will let you know when I'm done.

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Done — submitted.

transcript.speaker: Medha
transcript.text: Thanks. Final question: tell us about accessibility — what's your real-world approach beyond Lighthouse and axe?

meetingTopic: Interview: John Doe — Senior React Engineer

questions.competencyName: HTML5 semantic elements — landmark roles + heading hierarchy
questions.questionText: How do you approach structuring a complex single-page application with HTML5 landmark roles and heading hierarchy to ensure accessibility and SEO?
followUpHints: Can you give an example of when you might use a <nav> inside a <header> versus a standalone <nav>?
How do you handle dynamic content that changes the heading hierarchy?

questions.competencyName: ARIA — live regions, role=alert, aria-expanded, aria-controls
questions.questionText: Can you describe a scenario where you used ARIA live regions or role=alert, and how you ensured they worked correctly with screen readers?
followUpHints: How do you test that aria-expanded updates are announced properly?
What are common pitfalls with aria-controls?

questions.competencyName: Focus management — tabindex, focus trapping, skip links
questions.questionText: How do you manage focus in a modal dialog or a single-page app navigation? Walk me through your implementation choices for tabindex and focus trapping.
followUpHints: When would you use tabindex="-1" vs tabindex="0"?
How do you implement skip links and ensure they are visible on focus?

questions.competencyName: Forms — validation, error messages, aria-invalid, aria-describedby
questions.questionText: Describe your approach to building an accessible form with client-side validation. How do you connect error messages to inputs using ARIA?
followUpHints: How do you handle real-time validation vs on-submit validation?
What do you do for server-side errors that come back?

questions.competencyName: SEO — meta tags, Open Graph, structured data (JSON-LD)
questions.questionText: How do you structure meta tags and JSON-LD for a content-rich website to maximize SEO and social sharing? What trade-offs do you consider?
followUpHints: How do you handle dynamic Open Graph images?
What is your strategy for keeping structured data in sync with page content?

questions.competencyName: CSS Grid — grid-template-areas, auto-fit vs auto-fill, gap
questions.questionText: Can you explain the difference between auto-fit and auto-fill in CSS Grid, and give an example of when you would use each?
followUpHints: How do you use grid-template-areas for responsive layouts?
How do you handle gaps in older browsers?

questions.questionText: How do container queries differ from media queries? Describe a use case where container queries are the better solution.
followUpHints: What are the limitations of container queries currently?
How do you set up a container context?

questions.questionText: How do you use CSS custom properties to manage theme switching or component theming? What fallback strategies do you employ?
followUpHints: Have you used @property to define custom properties with types? When is that useful?
How do you handle inheritance issues?

questions.competencyName: CSS architecture — BEM, CUBE, naming conventions
questions.questionText: What CSS naming convention or architecture do you prefer for large-scale projects, and why? How do you handle specificity and maintainability?
followUpHints: What are the downsides of BEM?
How does CUBE CSS address some of those issues?

questions.competencyName: Responsive design — mobile-first, breakpoints, fluid typography
questions.questionText: Explain your process for building a mobile-first responsive layout. How do you choose breakpoints and implement fluid typography?
followUpHints: How do you handle complex tables or data grids on small screens?
What tools do you use to test responsive designs?

questions.competencyName: ES2023 — array findLast, toSorted, toReversed, with
questions.questionText: How do the new ES2023 array methods like toSorted and toReversed differ from their mutating counterparts? When would you use them?
followUpHints: How does findLast improve over find combined with reverse?
Are there any performance considerations?

questions.competencyName: Modules — import/export, dynamic import, tree shaking
questions.questionText: How do you leverage dynamic imports in a JavaScript application to improve performance? What impact does it have on tree shaking?
followUpHints: How do you handle loading states or errors with dynamic imports?
What are the trade-offs of using dynamic imports vs bundling everything?

questions.competencyName: Event loop — microtasks vs macrotasks, requestAnimationFrame
questions.questionText: Can you explain the difference between microtasks and macrotasks in the JavaScript event loop? How does requestAnimationFrame fit in?
followUpHints: Give an example of a situation where microtasks can cause performance issues.
How do you use requestAnimationFrame for smooth animations?

questions.competencyName: TypeScript — utility types, generics, type guards
questions.questionText: How do you use TypeScript generics and utility types to create reusable and type-safe components? Can you give an example of a custom type guard you've written?
followUpHints: When would you use Partial vs Pick?
How do you handle complex nested types?

questions.competencyName: Core Web Vitals — LCP, FID/INP, CLS measurement + optimization
questions.questionText: What strategies do you use to optimize Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) and Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) in a production web application?
followUpHints: How do you measure these metrics in real user monitoring?
What are common causes of poor CLS and how do you fix them?

questions.competencyName: Service workers — install, activate, fetch, cache strategies
questions.questionText: Describe a caching strategy you have implemented with service workers. How do you handle cache invalidation and updates?

liveTranscript.text: Sure, makes sense.

liveTranscript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
liveTranscript.text: Walk me through how you'd architect that.

liveTranscript.speaker: Test Candidate
liveTranscript.text: Yes.

liveTranscript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
liveTranscript.text: What was the hardest part of that project?

liveTranscript.speaker: Test Candidate
liveTranscript.text: Sure, makes sense.

liveTranscript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
liveTranscript.text: How would you handle scale on that system?

liveTranscript.speaker: Test Candidate
liveTranscript.text: We used Redis with a 60-second TTL for the hot keys and a dual-write to Postgres for any record that needed read-after-write. The tricky part was cache invalidation when the underlying record changed — we ended up using Postgres LISTEN/NOTIFY to push invalidation events into a Redis pub/sub channel, which kept the cache consistent within about 10ms.

liveTranscript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
liveTranscript.text: Have you done anything with caching there?

liveTranscript.speaker: Test Candidate
liveTranscript.text: I built that auth flow with NextAuth on the frontend and a custom JWT issuer on the backend. We used short-lived access tokens (15 min) with refresh rotation, and stored the refresh token in an httpOnly secure cookie scoped to the API subdomain. The biggest mistake we made early on was not implementing token theft detection — we added that later by tracking the last-used IP and family of refresh tokens.

liveTranscript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
liveTranscript.text: Walk me through how you'd architect that.

liveTranscript.speaker: Test Candidate
liveTranscript.text: For the data pipeline, we went with Apache Beam on Dataflow for stream processing because we needed exactly-once semantics across the Kafka source and the BigQuery sink. The watermark handling was painful — late events would arrive 30+ minutes after the window had closed, so we configured allowed lateness and side outputs to capture them in a separate table for backfill.

The tricky part was cache invalidation when the underlying record changed — we ended up using Postgres LISTEN/NOTIFY to push invalidation events into a Redis pub/sub channel, which kept the cache consistent within about 10ms.

We used short-lived access tokens (15 min) with refresh rotation, and stored the refresh token in an httpOnly secure cookie scoped to the API subdomain. header.domainFeedbackSummary: John demonstrated deep expertise in React, covering rendering patterns, performance optimization, state management, testing, and accessibility with clear, practical examples. He showed strong decision-making in choosing SSR vs SSG vs ISR, and explained memoization trade-offs honestly. His hands-on exercise was not observed, but his verbal answers were thorough. Weaknesses include limited Node.js depth (no streaming or database experience) and no GraphQL production experience beyond Apollo/Prisma. Overall, he is a strong senior candidate with a modern React skill set.
header.teachableSkillGapDetails: Node.js streaming (WebSockets, SSE) and GraphQL caching strategies could be strengthened.

competencies.feedbackDetails: Explained conditional rendering with ternary vs &&, including edge case with 0. Showed awareness of deeply nested conditions.
evidenceQuotes: The honest answer is most people overuse them.

competencies.feedbackDetails: Discussed CSS-in-JS trade-offs, theming, and performance concerns. Mentioned styled-components and Emotion.
Explained complex state with useState/useEffect, cleanup functions, and dependency management.
Stressed stable keys, warned against array index, and discussed reordering consequences.
evidenceQuotes: You also have to give each row a stable key, not the array index, because if the data reorders React will reuse the wrong DOM.

competencies.feedbackDetails: Recommended react-hook-form with zod for validation, discussed controlled vs uncontrolled, and accessibility.
Advocated integration tests with RTL, MSW for mocking, and avoiding implementation details.
evidenceQuotes: I avoid testing implementation details like the names of useState setters or specific class names.

competencies.feedbackDetails: Explained error boundaries catch render/lifecycle errors, not event handlers or async code. Suggested route-level and widget-level placement.
evidenceQuotes: Error boundaries catch errors in the render phase, lifecycle methods, and constructors of children below them.

competencies.feedbackDetails: Articulated clear decision criteria for SSR vs SSG vs ISR vs CSR, and mentioned RSC mixing.
evidenceQuotes: I think of it as a spectrum keyed off of two things: how often the content changes and how personalized it is.

competencies.feedbackDetails: Compared custom hooks vs HOCs, gave example of polymorphic Button, and discussed composition.
Described using Context + useReducer for forms/wizards, splitting dispatch provider to avoid re-renders.
evidenceQuotes: I'll wrap it in a context with a stable value so consumers don't re-render unnecessarily.

competencies.feedbackDetails: Explained useRef for DOM nodes, mutable values, and previous state tracking. Differentiated from regular variables.
evidenceQuotes: The key thing is mutating ref.current does NOT cause a re-render.

competencies.feedbackDetails: Gave nuanced advice: memoize only when profiler shows issue, warned against overuse.
evidenceQuotes: Wrapping every handler in useCallback is cargo culting and actually costs you memory.

competencies.feedbackDetails: Discussed React.lazy/Suspense for code splitting, loading states, and SEO implications.
Split server vs client state, recommended react-query for server state, Context for client, and Redux only for complex domains.
evidenceQuotes: Redux is overkill for most apps in 2026.

competencies.feedbackDetails: Mentioned environment variables, security considerations, and logging best practices.
Mentioned 12-factor app principles (config from env, stateless processes, logs as streams) but did not go deep.
evidenceQuotes: For a clean architecture I lean on the 12-factor principles — config from env, stateless processes, logs as event streams.

competencies.feedbackDetails: Discussed REST API design but not specifically file uploads or status codes in depth.
Mentioned fastify gives OpenAPI docs, but did not elaborate on Swagger/Postman or versioning.
Compared WebSockets vs SSE, mentioned socket.io, but no deep dive on reconnection or scaling.
evidenceQuotes: WebSockets when the protocol needs to be truly bidirectional... SSE when it's server-push-only.

competencies.feedbackDetails: Mentioned Prisma and Postgres, but did not discuss ORM vs raw SQL trade-offs or Redis caching.
Compared Express vs NestJS, mentioned fastify, but no deep comparison of middleware or DI.
evidenceQuotes: I've used Nest for one project but found it heavy for what was a small team.

competencies.feedbackDetails: Mentioned Apollo Server and Prisma, but did not compare Hasura or AppSync.
Briefly mentioned InMemoryCache and fetchPolicy, but no deep caching strategy or invalidation.

competencies.feedbackDetails: Mentioned dataloader for N+1, but no discussion of custom scalars or introspection.
evidenceQuotes: The N+1 problem was the main pain point — we fixed it with dataloader to batch.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Hi, thanks for joining today. Could you start by telling me a bit about your background and the React work you've been doing recently?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Sure. I've been working with React for about five years. Most recently at a fintech I led the rewrite of our trading dashboard from a CRA setup to a Next.js 14 app router project. We had a hard requirement around streaming the initial paint under 1.5 seconds even on mid-tier devices, so I leaned heavily on RSC and Suspense boundaries. Before that I spent two years on a design system team building reusable components published to a private npm registry, including a polymorphic Button using TypeScript generics and an Input that handled controlled and uncontrolled modes properly.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Good. Let's start with rendering patterns. Walk me through when you'd reach for SSR versus SSG versus client-only rendering, and where ISR fits in.

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: I think of it as a spectrum keyed off of two things: how often the content changes and how personalized it is. SSG when the content is the same for every user and changes rarely, like marketing pages or docs — you get the cheapest cache hit at the CDN edge. ISR is the same idea but with a TTL so editors don't have to redeploy; you trade staleness for ergonomics. SSR when the page is per-user or per-request — auth-gated dashboards, search result pages — you pay for compute on every request but you avoid client-side flicker. CSR when interactivity dominates and SEO doesn't matter, like a logged-in workspace. In Next 14 it gets blurrier because RSC lets you mix server components for the shell and client components for the interactive bits, which is usually the right answer for a real app.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Nice. How would you handle a list of 10,000 rows in React without killing performance?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Don't render 10,000 DOM nodes. Virtualize with react-window or TanStack Virtual — they render only the visible window plus a small overscan. You also have to give each row a stable key, not the array index, because if the data reorders React will reuse the wrong DOM. For the data side I'd memoize the row component with React.memo and pass a stable callback via useCallback so the row doesn't re-render on parent updates. If the rows are heavy I'd also profile with the React DevTools profiler to see if a context update is forcing a re-render and split the context if so.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Tell me about useMemo and useCallback. When are they worth it and when are they noise?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: The honest answer is most people overuse them. useMemo is worth it when the computation is genuinely expensive — a large sort, a tree flatten — or when the referential identity of the value matters because it's a dep of another hook or a memoized child. useCallback is the same: only useful if the function is passed to a memoized child or used as a hook dep. Wrapping every handler in useCallback is cargo culting and actually costs you memory. The mental model I use is: don't memoize until you have a profiler trace showing a real problem, except at the boundary of an obviously memoized component.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: What about state management in a real app? Redux, Zustand, react-query — how do you choose?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: I split state into server state and client state. Server state — anything that came from an API — goes in react-query or RTK Query because they handle caching, dedupe, background refetch, and stale-while-revalidate out of the box. For client state I default to useState colocated with the component. If two distant components need it I lift to a Context, and only reach for Zustand or Redux Toolkit when there's complex cross-cutting state with derived selectors. Redux is overkill for most apps in 2026, but it's still defensible if you need devtools time travel for a complex domain like a trading book or a CAD tool.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Let's go deeper on hooks. Explain useRef and the difference between useRef and a regular variable in a component.

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: A regular variable gets re-initialized on every render. useRef gives you a mutable container that persists across renders without triggering a re-render when you change it. Two common uses: holding a DOM node so you can imperatively focus an input, and stashing a mutable value like a setTimeout id or a previous-value snapshot for comparison. The key thing is mutating ref.current does NOT cause a re-render — if you want a re-render when something changes, that's state, not a ref.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Have you used Context with useReducer for state? When would you reach for that over plain useState?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Yes. The pattern shines when you have a state object with several related fields and the transitions are non-trivial — like a form with multi-step validation, or a wizard. The reducer documents the legal transitions in one place. I'll wrap it in a context with a stable value so consumers don't re-render unnecessarily, and I'll often split the dispatch into its own provider so components that only dispatch don't subscribe to state.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: What about error boundaries? Where do you place them and what do they catch versus not catch?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Error boundaries catch errors in the render phase, lifecycle methods, and constructors of children below them. They do not catch errors in event handlers, async code, or server-side rendering — for those you wrap with try/catch or use the onError callback. Placement-wise I put one at the route level so a crashed page doesn't take down the whole app, and a finer-grained one around risky widgets like a third-party charting library. The fallback should be useful — a retry button and a reset key, not just "Something went wrong."

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: How do you handle forms in React? Controlled vs uncontrolled, validation libraries?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: For anything non-trivial I use react-hook-form because it's uncontrolled by default which keeps re-renders to a minimum, and the resolver pattern plays well with zod schemas — I get one source of truth for the type and the runtime validation. For tiny one-or-two-field forms I'll just use controlled useState. Native HTML5 validation gets you a long way for required and type=email; I lean on it before reaching for a library. For accessibility I make sure each input has a real label, aria-invalid on error, and a live region for the error message.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Walk me through testing. RTL, Jest, what's your philosophy?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: I follow the testing trophy from Kent C. Dodds — heavy on integration, light on unit, and a small e2e layer with Playwright for critical paths like checkout or login. RTL queries by role and accessible name, which doubles as an accessibility check. I avoid testing implementation details like the names of useState setters or specific class names. For mocks I use MSW at the network boundary instead of mocking fetch directly, because that lets the same handlers run in tests and in Storybook. Coverage targets are a smell to me — I focus on whether the critical user flows are covered.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Tell me about TypeScript with React. Have you written generic components or used advanced types?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Yes. The polymorphic Button I mentioned is a good example — it takes an "as" prop that lets you render as a button, a link, or any element, and the rest of the props are typed against that element. That involves a ComponentPropsWithoutRef plus a generic with a default of "button". I use conditional types for things like making a prop required only when another prop is set — a discriminated union is usually cleaner though. tsconfig-wise I always turn on strict, noUncheckedIndexedAccess, and exactOptionalPropertyTypes. Those three catch the most real bugs.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Let's talk performance budgets. What metrics do you watch and how do you fix regressions?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: I anchor on the Core Web Vitals — LCP, CLS, INP since it replaced FID. For a React app, LCP is usually about how fast the hero element renders and whether it's blocked by JS hydration. I'll check the waterfall in Lighthouse or WebPageTest, look for render-blocking scripts, preload the LCP image, and split routes with React.lazy plus Suspense. CLS is usually missing dimensions on images or late-loading fonts — I fix that with width/height attributes and font-display swap with a sensible fallback metric override. INP is event handler latency — yieldy work with scheduler.yield, debounce expensive handlers, and move heavy compute to a worker if needed.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Accessibility — what's your real-world approach beyond axe and Lighthouse?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Automated tools catch maybe 30% of issues. Real wins come from keyboard testing — tab through the whole flow, make sure focus is visible and lands where you'd expect, that modal traps focus and returns it on close. Screen reader testing with VoiceOver or NVDA at least once per major flow. Color contrast against actual content, not just buttons. ARIA only when there isn't a native element — a button is always better than a div with role=button. I also try to ship a "reduced motion" code path because the prefers-reduced-motion media query is easy to honor and a real accessibility win.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Security on the frontend — what's on your radar?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: XSS first — never dangerouslySetInnerHTML on user content without sanitizing through DOMPurify; React's default escaping handles most cases. CSP headers set tight, with nonces if you have inline scripts. CSRF — same-site cookies and tokens for state-changing requests. Don't store auth tokens in localStorage if you can avoid it; httpOnly cookies are safer against XSS exfiltration. For third-party scripts I use SRI hashes. And I never log PII to the browser console or analytics.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Tooling — Vite vs webpack, and what does your lint/format setup look like?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Vite for new projects — dev startup is night and day, native ESM is the right primitive. Webpack still wins for very complex enterprise pipelines with custom loaders. For lint I use ESLint with typescript-eslint, the react and react-hooks plugins, and jsx-a11y. Prettier for formatting, with the eslint-config-prettier wired in so they don't fight. Husky plus lint-staged for pre-commit hooks. I also run typecheck in CI as a separate job so PR feedback is fast.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Let's pivot to Node briefly. Have you built REST APIs in Node? What framework?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Yes. Most of my Node work has been Express historically, and recently fastify because the schema-driven validation with JSON Schema is fast and gives you OpenAPI docs essentially for free. For a clean architecture I lean on the 12-factor principles — config from env, stateless processes, logs as event streams. I've used Nest for one project but found it heavy for what was a small team. I'd reach for it again only if I were on a 15-plus engineer org that needed the discipline.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Have you worked with WebSockets or SSE?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Both. WebSockets when the protocol needs to be truly bidirectional — a live collaboration cursor or a chat. SSE when it's server-push-only — a notifications feed, a deployment progress tracker — because it gives you reconnection and event IDs for free over plain HTTP, and it plays nicer with HTTP/2 and corporate proxies. I've used socket.io for the WebSocket layer when I needed rooms and reconnection out of the box.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: What about GraphQL — have you used it in production?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Yes, on a product catalog. We used Apollo Server on the backend with a Postgres-backed Prisma schema, and Apollo Client on the React side with the InMemoryCache. The wins were obvious for the frontend — request exactly what you need, no overfetching, typed end-to-end. The N+1 problem was the main pain point — we fixed it with dataloader to batch. Caching strategy was a per-type policy in the InMemoryCache; we used fetchPolicy 'cache-and-network' for the hot paths.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Last question — what's your take on Module Federation and micro-frontends?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: I'm skeptical by default. Most teams that adopt micro-frontends do it for organizational reasons not technical ones, and they end up duplicating runtime, fighting version skew, and slowing the boot time of the host app. Module Federation is a fine tool if you genuinely need independent deploy cadences across teams and the shared chunks are well-managed. I've shipped one production MFE with five domains using Module Federation 2, shared React singletons, and a tight contract on the design system version. It worked, but the operational complexity was real — versioning, type sharing, runtime errors when a remote was unavailable.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: Great. Anything you want to ask me or anything we didn't cover?

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Two quick things — what does your release cadence look like, and how does the team handle on-call rotations for the frontend? Also curious how design and engineering collaborate at the spec stage.

transcript.speaker: Sid Chatterjee
transcript.text: We ship multiple times a day behind feature flags, on-call is two-week rotations shared across full-stack engineers, and we have a weekly design-engineering sync where we triage the next two sprints together. Thanks for the time — we'll be in touch this week.

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Thanks, appreciated the conversation.

transcript.speaker: Medha
transcript.text: 👋 Hello and welcome! I'm Medha, your AI interviewer. This conversation will be analyzed by an AI assistant and a structured probe form will be generated for the hiring panel. The session is being recorded and transcribed for that purpose. By continuing, you consent to this process. Data retention: 30 days.

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Got it, thanks — happy to proceed.

transcript.speaker: Medha
transcript.text: Question 1. Walk us through when you'd reach for SSR vs SSG vs CSR vs ISR, and where RSC fits in.

transcript.speaker: Medha
transcript.text: Question 2. How would you render a list of 10,000 rows in React without killing performance? What about useMemo and useCallback — when are they worth it?

transcript.speaker: Medha
transcript.text: Question 3. For the hands-on round, please use this CoderPad link: Shopping Cart Exercise . You have 20 minutes — I'll see your code at the end.

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Opening it now — will let you know when I'm done.

transcript.speaker: Test Candidate
transcript.text: Done — submitted.

transcript.speaker: Medha
transcript.text: Thanks. Final question: tell us about accessibility — what's your real-world approach beyond Lighthouse and axe?
